bins.name: Study Full Nameplate Capacity
bins.desc: UCS supports requiring that interconnection studies for hybrid facilities use full nameplate capacity for fault analysis. The filing argues that export-limited capacity is appropriate for thermal screening but not for fault conditions, because power limiting controls have inherent delays and cannot prevent inadvertent export. UCS draws on DER interconnection experience and IEEE 2800 to show this gap applies to transmission-connected systems as well.
quotes: capabilities to limit injections or withdrawals are not instantaneous and are not error-proof.
the full nameplate capacity must still be used when studying fault conditions to identify power quality impacts.

the drafting and adoption of rules that presume tight control and a narrow range of performance from conventional generation with co-located loads will need to be based on clearly stated and explicitly measured standards or limits.

bins.name: Hybrid Facility Injection and Withdrawal Rights
bins.desc: UCS accepts the concept of net-based injection and withdrawal rights for hybrid facilities but argues the study process must still separately evaluate gross load and gross generation amounts. Power limiting systems are not instantaneous, so relying on net rights alone understates the range of grid impacts a hybrid facility can cause. Rules must be grounded in explicit, measurable performance standards.
quotes: capabilities to limit injections or withdrawals are not instantaneous and are not error-proof.
the full nameplate capacity must still be used when studying fault conditions to identify power quality impacts.

the drafting and adoption of rules that presume tight control and a narrow range of performance from conventional generation with co-located loads will need to be based on clearly stated and explicitly measured standards or limits.

system protection facilities must: 1. Ensure that if the facility exceeds the allowed limits, its import or export is returned to the allowed limits within a fixed time period, defined by the TSO. 2. During the inadvertent export or import event, maintain the voltage at the point of interconnection within limits defined by the TSO.

bins.name: Technical Protection Requirements
bins.desc: UCS recommends that system protection facilities enforce both duration and voltage limits on inadvertent export or import events, not just capacity limits. The filing calls on FERC to convene a technical expert workgroup to set minimum requirements, and warns that rules should not rely solely on financial penalties as a substitute for engineering requirements.
quotes: A future set of proposed rules forthcoming from the Commission should be informed by technical considerations and not be limited to a financial penalty framework.

system protection facilities must: 1. Ensure that if the facility exceeds the allowed limits, its import or export is returned to the allowed limits within a fixed time period, defined by the TSO. 2. During the inadvertent export or import event, maintain the voltage at the point of interconnection within limits defined by the TSO.

we recommend that the Commission convene a workgroup of technical experts to develop such requirements. These requirements should be responsive to available technical capability and should prioritize reliability while avoiding excessive costs to developers and consumers.

bins.name: Clear Curtailment and Dispatch Rules
bins.desc: UCS supports expedited interconnection for curtailable and dispatchable large loads but says the principle is unworkable without explicit rules specifying duration and quantity of curtailment commitments. The filing recommends designating both supply and load from hybrid customers as non-firm and subject to economic dispatch, consistent with existing Order 888 practices for non-firm transmission service.
quotes: the Commission should propose rules that both supply (in the case of a hybrid interconnection) and the load will need to commit to respond to operator dispatch instructions. That commitment should indicate both the duration and the quantity of the response, in either megawatts or percentage of the injection and withdrawal rights.

Any forthcoming rule from the Commission must include criteria for determining the amount and the duration of curtailment and/or dispatch to avoid controversy and delay in the adoption of this principle.

The most direct approach compatible with existing grid operator dispatch is to have both the supply and demand from such interconnection customers be designated as non-firm and subject to economic dispatch.

bins.name: Expedited Study for Curtailable Loads
bins.desc: UCS supports expediting interconnection studies for curtailable and dispatchable loads but insists that any rulemaking define the specific terms of curtailment to avoid disputes and implementation delays. A clear non-firm economic dispatch designation is the recommended path.
quotes: the Commission should propose rules that both supply (in the case of a hybrid interconnection) and the load will need to commit to respond to operator dispatch instructions. That commitment should indicate both the duration and the quantity of the response, in either megawatts or percentage of the injection and withdrawal rights.

Any forthcoming rule from the Commission must include criteria for determining the amount and the duration of curtailment and/or dispatch to avoid controversy and delay in the adoption of this principle.

The most direct approach compatible with existing grid operator dispatch is to have both the supply and demand from such interconnection customers be designated as non-firm and subject to economic dispatch.

bins.name: Large Load Cost Causation
bins.desc: UCS strongly supports assigning 100 percent of identified network upgrade costs to the large loads that cause them. The filing documents that over $4 billion in connection costs were added to ratebases in 2024 across seven states, and explains how the current regulatory gap allows data center costs to be socialized across all retail customers while revenues from those customers are credited only to the largest customer class.
quotes: we recommend that the Commission convene a workgroup of technical experts to develop such requirements. These requirements should be responsive to available technical capability and should prioritize reliability while avoiding excessive costs to developers and consumers.
Costs for transmission connections and network extensions required by new large loads have already added over $4 billion to the ratebase of utilities in 2024 from construction in just seven states.
The existing rules and practices allow the worst outcome: the rapidly rising costs for data centers' needs are spread across all the utilities' customers.

Under existing practices the revenues from new large load customers are recognized for the largest customer class, but the costs caused by those customers are allocated to all customers. This is not the application of the 'Bonbright' cost-causation principle that is so often cited in utility cost allocation debates.

this proposed Principle of assigning costs for all upgrades identified in the interconnection studies for large loads will address a regulatory gap and correctly apply the widely cited principle of cost causation to significant costs presently misallocated to all consumers.

bins.name: Network Upgrade Cost Allocation Gap
bins.desc: UCS documents a structural gap in which transmission upgrade costs driven by large loads flow through FERC formula rates into state retail rate cases, where they are spread across all customers rather than assigned to the causing customer class. The filing uses PJM data to illustrate the scale and calls Principle 8 a correct application of cost causation to close this gap.
quotes: Costs for transmission connections and network extensions required by new large loads have already added over $4 billion to the ratebase of utilities in 2024 from construction in just seven states.
The existing rules and practices allow the worst outcome: the rapidly rising costs for data centers' needs are spread across all the utilities' customers.

Under existing practices the revenues from new large load customers are recognized for the largest customer class, but the costs caused by those customers are allocated to all customers. This is not the application of the 'Bonbright' cost-causation principle that is so often cited in utility cost allocation debates.

this proposed Principle of assigning costs for all upgrades identified in the interconnection studies for large loads will address a regulatory gap and correctly apply the widely cited principle of cost causation to significant costs presently misallocated to all consumers.

bins.name: PJM Data Center Cost Evidence
bins.desc: UCS cites PJM local planning data to identify 150 examples of utilities connecting new customers of 20 MW or more at a combined cost to consumers of more than $5 billion, illustrating the scale of the cost causation problem in the PJM region.
quotes: Costs for transmission connections and network extensions required by new large loads have already added over $4 billion to the ratebase of utilities in 2024 from construction in just seven states.

bins.name: Explicit Performance Standards Needed
bins.desc: UCS argues that rules relying on tight operational control of co-located loads and generation must rest on clearly stated, explicitly measured performance standards. Existing standards for thermal generators on the bulk power system have not required the same precision, so new rules should establish measurable benchmarks before imposing compliance obligations.
quotes: the drafting and adoption of rules that presume tight control and a narrow range of performance from conventional generation with co-located loads will need to be based on clearly stated and explicitly measured standards or limits.

bins.name: Technical Rules Over Penalty Reliance
bins.desc: UCS cautions against designing the protection framework primarily around financial penalties for unauthorized injections or withdrawals, arguing that rules should be grounded in technical requirements and that a penalty-only approach is not sufficient to ensure reliability.
quotes: A future set of proposed rules forthcoming from the Commission should be informed by technical considerations and not be limited to a financial penalty framework.

bins.name: Regulatory Cost Causation Gap
bins.desc: UCS identifies a specific regulatory gap in which the revenues from large load customers are credited only to the largest customer class in retail rate cases while the transmission costs they cause are spread across all customers, violating the Bonbright cost causation principle.
quotes: Under existing practices the revenues from new large load customers are recognized for the largest customer class, but the costs caused by those customers are allocated to all customers. This is not the application of the 'Bonbright' cost-causation principle that is so often cited in utility cost allocation debates.
